question: Define the interior of a set A in a metric space (X, d).
expectedPoints: A point x ∈ A is an interior point if there exists an ε > 0 such that the open ball B(x, ε) is entirely contained in A.
The interior of A, denoted int(A), is the set of all interior points of A.
The interior is the largest open set contained within A.

question: Explain the relationship between the closure of a set and its limit points in a metric space. How is the closure of a set A defined?
expectedPoints: The closure of A, denoted cl(A) or Ā, is the intersection of all closed sets containing A.
The closure of A is equal to the union of A and the set of all its limit points (also called the derived set).
A point x is in the closure of A if and only if every open ball centered at x contains at least one point of A.

question: Prove that the closure of a set A in a metric space is a closed set. Furthermore, prove that it is the smallest closed set containing A.
expectedPoints: To prove cl(A) is closed, show its complement is open: if x is not in cl(A), there exists an ε-neighborhood of x that does not intersect A, and this neighborhood can be shown to not intersect cl(A) either.
To prove it is the smallest, assume F is any closed set containing A. Since F is closed and contains A, it must contain all limit points of A, and therefore must contain cl(A).
Thus, cl(A) is a subset of every closed set that contains A, making it the smallest such set.
